title: Permissions & Prohibitions
learningGoal: The learner will utilize the Te-form to ask for permission (~te mo ii desu ka) and state strict rules or prohibitions (~te wa ikemasen).
instructions: This lesson builds directly on your knowledge of the Te-form. By adding specific phrases to the end of a Te-form verb, you can ask for permission or warn someone against doing something.
conceptTitle: May I? And You Must Not!

lessonContent.sectionTitle: Asking for Permission (~te mo ii desu ka)
lessonContent.bodyText: To ask 'May I do X?', take the Te-form of the verb and add 'mo ii desu ka'. 'Ii' means 'good', so literally you are asking 'Even if I do this, is it good?'

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: しゃしんを とっても いいですか。
targetLanguageExamples.transliteration: Shashin wo totte mo ii desu ka.
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: May I take a photo? (Torimasu -> Totte)

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: ここに すわっても いいですか。
targetLanguageExamples.transliteration: Koko ni suwatte mo ii desu ka.

lessonContent.bodyText: To say 'You must not do X', use the Te-form and add 'wa ikemasen'. This is a very strong prohibition used for rules, laws, or strict warnings.

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: ここで しゃしんを とっては いけません。
targetLanguageExamples.transliteration: Koko de shashin wo totte wa ikemasen.
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: You must not take photos here.

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: びょういんで たばこを すっては いけません。
targetLanguageExamples.transliteration: Byouin de tabako wo sutte wa ikemasen.
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: You must not smoke in the hospital. (Suimasu -> Sutte)

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: けいたいでんわを つかっては いけません。
targetLanguageExamples.transliteration: Keitai denwa wo tsukatte wa ikemasen.
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: You must not use a mobile phone. (Tsukaimasu -> Tsukatte)

lessonContent.bodyText: In the phrase '~te wa ikemasen', the 'wa' is written using the hiragana 'ha' (は), exactly the same as the topic marker 'wa'. Make sure to pronounce it 'wa', not 'ha'.

If someone asks for permission and you want to say no, saying '~te wa ikemasen' might sound too strict like a police officer. Instead, you can simply say 'Sumimasen, chotto...' (Sorry, it's a little...) or 'Dame desu' (It is forbidden/no good).

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: A: はいっても いいですか。
targetLanguageExamples.transliteration: A: Haitte mo ii desu ka.

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: B: すみません、だめです。

learningObjective: The learner will construct sentences expressing permission and prohibition using the Te-form, recognizing appropriate social contexts for strong versus soft refusals.

examples.targetText: びじゅつかんで しゃしんを とっては いけません。みても いいです。
examples.transliteration: Bijutsukan de shashin wo totte wa ikemasen. Mite mo ii desu.
examples.baseTranslation: You must not take photos in the museum. You may look.
examples.explanation: Contrasts a prohibition (totte wa ikemasen) with a permission (mite mo ii desu).

importantRules.ruleText: Do not use '~te wa ikemasen' to give advice to a friend. It is too strong. It is used for rules, laws, and danger.
importantRules.exceptionNotes: A teacher might say it to a student, or a sign might have it written.

importantRules.ruleText: The particle 'wa' in '~te wa ikemasen' is written with the character は (ha).

cultureContext.contextText: Japan has many unwritten rules about public spaces. For example, talking on a mobile phone on a train is generally considered 'Dame' (forbidden/frowned upon), even if it's not technically illegal.
dosList: Always ask 'shashin wo totte mo ii desu ka?' before taking pictures inside a shop or of a person.

summary: To ask for permission, use Te-form + mo ii desu ka. To grant permission, say ~te mo ii desu. To state a strict rule or prohibition, use Te-form + wa ikemasen. For a softer refusal, use 'Sumimasen, dame desu'.

checkpointQuestions.questionText: How do you ask 'May I drink?' (Drink = Nomimasu)
checkpointQuestions.expectedAnswer: のんでも いいですか (Nonde mo ii desu ka)

checkpointQuestions.questionText: How do you say 'You must not enter' (Enter = Hairimasu)?
checkpointQuestions.expectedAnswer: はいってはいけません (Haitte wa ikemasen)

checkpointQuestions.questionText: How do you say 'It is not allowed / No good'?

adminCorrectAnswerSet.conceptSummary: High-density block on social permissions and rules utilizing the Te-form.

checkpointAnswers.q1: のんでも いいですか
checkpointAnswers.q2: はいってはいけません

adminCorrectAnswerSet.expectedUnderstanding: Learner must seamlessly attach permission/prohibition suffixes to previously learned Te-form verbs.

feedbackRules.onPass: Excellent! You can now navigate rules and manners in Japanese society.
feedbackRules.onFail: Review the Te-form from Block 2. If you don't know the Te-form, you cannot build these new sentences.
feedbackRules.onPartial: Good start. Pay close attention to the pronunciation of 'wa ikemasen'.

audioAssets.audioTranscript: しゃしんを とっても いいですか。ここに すわっても いいですか。はい、いいですよ。ここで しゃしんを とっては いけません。びょういんで たばこを すっては いけません。けいたいでんわを つかっては いけません。はいっても いいですか。すみません、だめです。

commonMistakes: Pronouncing 'ha' instead of 'wa' in 'te wa ikemasen'.
